{{Chess Opening Theory/Position |Indian defence |parent=[[../|Queen's pawn opening]] |responses=
*[[/2. c4|2. c4]] *[[/2. Nf3|2. Nf3 · Knights variation]] *[[/2. Bg5|2. Bg5 · Trompowsky attack]] *[[/2. Bf4|2. Bf4 · Accelerated London/Indian]] }} == 1...Nf6 · Indian defence == Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1. d4. Black doesn't commit a pawn to the centre yet. They may yet do so, or they can fight for the centre in the hypermodern way: controlling it from afar with pieces, allowing White to construct their pawn centre but undermining it later with timely pawn advances. This move gives White lots of leeway to either develop pieces or take more space in the centre. Openings tend to be flexible with lots of potential for transpositions. === Take space in the centre === White would like two pawns in the centre if possible. [[/2. c4|'''2. c4''']] is the '''main line''': White takes more space and controls the important d5 square. After 2. c4, White can develop their knight behind their pawn line and perhaps play e4. Black can still choose to contest this centre with pawns with 2...d5, 2...c5 (Benoni), or 2...e5 (Budapest), but more typically they move their pawns only as necessary to develop their bishops: 2...e6, 2...g6, 2...d6 or 2...b6. White can't play e4 and keep the pawn there ([[/2. e4|'''2. e4?!''']] is called the '''Omega gambit''' and loses a pawn to 2...Nxe4). White can prepare 3. e4 with [[/2. Nc3|'''2. Nc3''']] or [[/2. f3|'''2. f3''']], which usually compels Black to play 2...d5 to keep control of e4. === Develop a piece === Alternatively, White can prioritise piece development. [[/2. Nf3|'''2. Nf3''']], the '''Knights variation''' is the chief sideline. It's a flexible move: White may still play c4 afterwards but first gets more information on how Black intends to play. [[/2. Nc3|'''2. Nc3''']], prevents c4 but, as noted above, prepares e4. After 2...d5 (controlling e4) the main continuations are 3. Bg5, the '''Richter-Veresov attack''', or 3. Bf4, the '''Jobava-Rapport London'''. The bishop has two main candidate squares to develop to. [[/2. Bg5|'''2. Bg5''']], the '''Trompowsky attack''', pressures Black's knight. White has hopes of trading it to double Black's pawns. [[/2. Bf4|'''2. Bf4''']] plays the '''Accelerated London system''' into Black's Indian defence. Play may [[Chess Opening Theory/1. d4/1...Nf6/2. Bf4/2...d5|transpose with 2...d5]], but the Indian defence has original ways to meet it without ...d5. === Uncommon moves === *[[/2. e3|'''2. e3''']] plays in [[Chess Opening Theory/1. d4/1...Nf6/2. Nf3/2...d5/3. e3|'''Colle system''']]-style against the Indian defence. Transposes if Black plays ...d5. *[[/2. g3|'''2. g3''']], the '''Tartakower attack''', prepares to fianchetto the bishop. === Strange moves=== *[[/2. d5 |'''2. d5?!''']], the '''Pawn push variation'''. Since Black didn't play d5, White can advance their pawn, guarded by their queen. After 2...e6 3. dxe6 fxe6, White loses their central pawn. *[[/2. f4|'''2. f4?!''']], the '''Canard opening'''. *[[/2. Nd2|'''2. Nd2?!''']], hoping to support e4 without preventing c4, should be met with 2...d4, but 2...e4?! is the unsound '''Lazard gambit'''. *[[/2. g4|'''2. g4?!''']], the '''Bronstein gambit''', hopes to tempt Black's knight away from controlling e4 by offering a free flank pawn. == Theory table == {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} '''1.d4 Nf6''' {| class="wikitable" |- ! !! 2 !! 3 !! 4 !! 5 !! 6 !!
